Okazaki’s experiments
Expt I – pulse labeling
put synchronized cells in medium
all start DNA replication
add 3H-labeled dNTPs
let them continue DNA synthesis
incorporating labeled nucleotides
collect DNA, centrifuge = separate by size
where is radioactive (newly made) DNA?

parental DNA
new DNA synthesized before 3H-dNTPs added
DNA synthesized with 3H-dNTPs
Found both large and small DNA fragments
containing radioactive nucleotides

actual data
after 60 seconds, have both
small and large pieces of
radioactively tagged DNA

Okazaki’s experiments
Expt II – pulse-chase
put synchronized cells in medium
all start DNA replication
add 3H-labeled dNTPs
let them continue DNA synthesis incorporating labeled nucleotides
remove 3H-dNTPs, only “cold” (nonradioactive) dNTPs available
continue replication = chase
collect DNA, centrifuge = separate by size
where is radioactively labeled DNA made during the “pulse”?
short 3H-DNA fragments are converted into long pieces

could be made during chase

leading strand = continuous synthesis
lagging strand = discontinuous synthesis

Requirements for strand elongation
1)  A free 3’-OH at the end of the strand to be extended
2)  A hydrogen-bonded (base-paired) nucleotide at the end of
the strand to be elongated
3)  An unpaired base on the adjacent nucleotide in the template
strand
What if DNA polymerase adds the wrong nucleotide?
Can’t continue if base isn’t base-paired to template strand!

So how does it ever get started?
DNA polymerases can do strand elongation, but NOT strand initiation

RNA polymerases can do strand initiation
in prokaryotes, origin binding proteins recruit RNA Polymerase
makes first primer
all other primers = primase, recruited by helicase
